_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS. They are purpose-built stand-ins that demonstrate the import format. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests (D-SR11). See SyntheticFlag field on every item.
_import_note: Fields map 1:1 to LSAT Item notetype fields (notetypes.md). 'SkillTag' may contain space-separated skill:: tags. 'SyntheticFlag' must be 'SYNTHETIC' for all items in this file.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All registered voters who showed identification were allowed to vote. Since Maria was allowed to vote, she must have shown identification.
items.Stem: The reasoning above is flawed because it
items.ChoiceA: assumes that only registered voters would want to vote
items.ChoiceB: treats a sufficient condition as a necessary condition
items.ChoiceC: concludes that Maria is a registered voter without sufficient evidence
items.ChoiceD: fails to consider that some voters may have been turned away
items.ChoiceE: relies on an unverified premise about voter identification requirements
items.WhyWrongA: The argument says nothing about who wants to vote — this is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Showing ID is sufficient to vote, but the argument treats it as necessary: because she voted, she must have shown ID. This reverses the conditional.
items.WhyWrongC: The argument doesn't conclude anything about registration status.
items.WhyWrongD: The argument is not about who was turned away; this is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongE: The premise is stipulated as given, not unverified in context.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Studies show that cities with more libraries have lower crime rates than cities with fewer libraries. Therefore, building more libraries will reduce crime.
items.Stem: The argument is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
items.ChoiceA: overstates the magnitude of the relationship between libraries and crime
items.ChoiceB: fails to consider that crime rates vary significantly by season
items.ChoiceC: mistakes a correlation for a causal relationship
items.ChoiceD: assumes that all libraries provide the same services
items.ChoiceE: ignores the possibility that crime rates have been declining nationally
items.WhyWrongA: The argument does not make claims about magnitude — this misidentifies the flaw.
items.WhyWrongB: Seasonal variation is unrelated to the argument's reasoning structure.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The argument observes a correlation (more libraries, lower crime) and infers a causal direction (libraries cause less crime) without ruling out alternative explanations.
items.WhyWrongD: Library services are not relevant to this reasoning flaw.
items.WhyWrongE: National trends could explain the correlation, but this choice describes a missing premise, not the flaw — close but less precise than C.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] I interviewed five students at a local university who all reported studying fewer than four hours per week. Clearly, university students today are not putting in adequate study time.
items.Stem: The argument above is most vulnerable to which of the following criticisms?
items.ChoiceA: It assumes that adequate study time is the same for all students.
items.ChoiceB: It draws a conclusion about all university students from a small, potentially unrepresentative sample.
items.ChoiceC: It fails to specify what constitutes adequate study time.
items.ChoiceD: It does not consider that some students may work part-time jobs.
items.ChoiceE: It presupposes that studying is the primary determinant of academic success.
items.WhyWrongA: The argument's flaw is about sample size, not about the definition of 'adequate' — though A is a real consideration, it is not the primary logical error.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Five students at one university cannot support a claim about all university students — hasty generalization from an unrepresentative sample.
items.WhyWrongC: Lack of definition is a peripheral concern; the main flaw is the generalization.
items.WhyWrongD: Part-time work is out of scope for the argument's logical structure.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument makes no claims about determinants of academic success.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The new transit line will reduce commute times for workers traveling downtown. Therefore, workers will arrive at work less stressed and will be more productive.
items.Stem: The argument depends on the assumption that
items.ChoiceA: the transit line will be completed on schedule and within budget
items.ChoiceB: reduced commute time leads to less stress for commuters
items.ChoiceC: productivity is the most important measure of worker performance
items.ChoiceD: downtown workers currently experience significant commute delays
items.ChoiceE: the transit line will be used by a majority of downtown workers
items.WhyWrongA: Construction logistics are not assumed in moving from shorter commutes to less stress — out of scope for this step.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The argument moves from 'shorter commute' to 'less stressed' — it must assume this connection holds. Negate it: 'shorter commutes do not reduce stress' → the conclusion collapses. Assumption Negation test passes.
items.WhyWrongC: The argument does not make productivity a primary metric; this is an irrelevant comparison.
items.WhyWrongD: Current delays are not assumed; the argument could hold even if delays are minor.
items.WhyWrongE: Usage rate is not required for the conclusion — even if only some workers use it, the logic still holds for them.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Sales of electric vehicles have increased every year for the past decade, and consumer awareness campaigns about climate change have also grown over the same period. The campaigns must be responsible for the rise in electric vehicle sales.
items.Stem: Which of the following is an assumption required by this argument?
items.ChoiceA: Electric vehicles are the most effective way to reduce carbon emissions.
items.ChoiceB: No other factors contributed significantly to the rise in electric vehicle sales.
items.ChoiceC: Consumer awareness campaigns are sponsored by electric vehicle manufacturers.
items.ChoiceD: The rise in electric vehicle sales is a desirable outcome for policymakers.
items.ChoiceE: Climate change awareness has increased more rapidly than electric vehicle sales.
items.WhyWrongA: Effectiveness of EVs for emissions reduction is out of scope; the argument is about the cause of their sales.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. To infer that the campaigns caused the sales increase, the argument must assume that no other explanation (falling battery costs, government subsidies, etc.) is responsible. Negation: 'Other factors did contribute significantly' → the correlation could be coincidental → conclusion fails.
items.WhyWrongC: Sponsorship is irrelevant to the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongD: Policy desirability is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongE: Relative growth rates are not assumed in the argument.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All certified organic farms must submit annual soil reports. Greenfield Farm has not submitted a soil report this year.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
items.ChoiceA: Greenfield Farm does not grow any organic produce.
items.ChoiceB: Greenfield Farm is not currently a certified organic farm.
items.ChoiceC: Greenfield Farm will lose its certification next year.
items.ChoiceD: Most certified organic farms submit their reports on time.
items.ChoiceE: The soil report requirement is new this year.
items.WhyWrongA: Growing organic produce does not require certification — a farm can grow organic produce without being certified. Out of scope.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Via contrapositive: If a farm does not submit a soil report → it is not a certified organic farm. Greenfield has not submitted → Greenfield is not certified. Valid deductive inference.
items.WhyWrongC: Future consequences (losing certification) go beyond what the stimulus supports — too extreme / not entailed.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Most farms' is a quantifier claim not supported by the stimulus, which says only 'all certified farms must submit.'
items.WhyWrongE: The newness of the requirement is not stated or inferable from the premises.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Some of the paintings in the museum were donated by private collectors. All paintings donated by private collectors have been authenticated. No painting that has been authenticated is available for loan.
items.Stem: Which of the following can be properly concluded from the statements above?
items.ChoiceA: All paintings in the museum have been authenticated.
items.ChoiceB: None of the paintings in the museum are available for loan.
items.ChoiceC: Some paintings in the museum are not available for loan.
items.ChoiceD: Some authenticated paintings were donated by private collectors.
items.ChoiceE: No private collector has donated a painting to any other museum.
items.WhyWrongA: Only some paintings were donated; we cannot conclude all are authenticated — too extreme.
items.WhyWrongB: We know only that some paintings are not available for loan (those donated by collectors); we cannot conclude none are — too extreme.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Some paintings → donated by collectors → authenticated → not available for loan. Via chained inference, some paintings are not available for loan. 'Some' claims survive the chain.
items.WhyWrongD: This reverses the direction: we know collector donations → authenticated, not that authenticated → collector donation. Reversal trap.
items.WhyWrongE: Other museums are entirely out of scope.
